title: Chapter 4 Master Quiz
description: Round and round! Test your knowledge of For loops, While loops, and flow control.

questions.question: What does the DRY principle stand for?
questions.explanation: DRY means you should avoid copying and pasting the exact same code repeatedly.

questions.question: Which type of loop runs AS LONG AS a specific condition remains true?
questions.explanation: A while loop continuously executes its block while its condition is true.

questions.question: In a `while(condition) { ... }` loop, when is the condition checked?
options: Before every single iteration (including the very first one).
questions.explanation: The condition is evaluated before the block runs, every single time. If it is false initially, the loop never runs.

questions.question:
How many times will this loop run?
`int x = 0; while (x < 5) { x++; }`
questions.explanation: It runs when x is 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. That is 5 iterations.

questions.question: If you forget to update the tracker variable inside a while loop, it causes an infinite loop.
questions.explanation: If the variable never changes, the condition never turns false, so it loops forever.

questions.question: Which operator is commonly used as a shortcut to add 1 to a variable?
questions.explanation: The increment operator `++` increases an integer's value by 1.

questions.question: What is the great advantage of a `for` loop over a `while` loop?
options: It packages initialization, condition, and update all on one concise line.
questions.explanation: Instead of spreading out the count tracker logic, a `for` loop groups it all in the parentheses.

questions.question: What separates the three sections in a `for` loop's declaration?
questions.explanation: It must be semicolons: `for (int i=0; i<10; i++)`.

questions.question: What is the standard name used for the tracker variable inside a `for` loop?
questions.explanation: By convention, programmers use `i` (short for index or iterator).

questions.question: What will this do: `for(int i = 5; i > 0; i--) { print(i); }`?
questions.explanation: It starts at 5, prints, subtracts 1. When it reaches 1, it prints. Then it becomes 0. `0 > 0` is false, so it stops before printing 0.

questions.question: If you want a loop to STOP immediately and exit the loop block forever, which keyword do you use?
questions.explanation: The `break` keyword instantly snaps execution out of the current loop entirely.

questions.question: If you want a loop to SKIP the rest of the current iteration, but jump back to the top and KEEP looping, which keyword do you use?
questions.explanation: The `continue` keyword abandons the current cycle and skips to the next cycle.

questions.question: A `continue` statement destroys the loop and prevents any future iterations from running.
questions.explanation: No, `continue` only destroys the *current* iteration. It proceeds to the next loops.

questions.question: In a `for(int i=1; i<=3; i++)` loop, if we put `if(i==2){continue;} print(i);`, what numbers print?
questions.explanation: When i=1, it prints 1. When i=2, it hits `continue` (which skips the print command). When i=3, it prints 3.

questions.question: What is the danger of an infinite loop?
options: It will freeze your program and consume all memory/CPU.
questions.explanation: The app freezes because it is stuck processing an endless cycle of tasks.

questions.question: The three parts of a `for` loop are Initialization, _____, and Update.
questions.explanation: The middle part is the Condition (e.g., `i < 10`).

questions.question: What results from this: `int n = 0; while(false) { n++; }`?
questions.explanation: Since the condition is `false` from the very start, the loop is entirely skipped.

questions.question: Can you put an `if` statement *inside* a `for` loop?
options: Only if it's a while loop.
questions.explanation: Yes, you can nest any logic (ifs, other loops, etc.) inside the body of a loop.

questions.question: Infinite loops are always bad and should never exist in programming.
questions.explanation: Sometimes they are intentional! Real-time applications like video game engines rely on intentional infinite loops to process frames continuously until the app is closed.

questions.question: What happens here: `for (int i=10; i>0; i++)`?
questions.explanation: It starts at 10 and adds 1 (`i++`). So it goes 11, 12, 13. The condition `i > 0` will ALWAYS be true, so it's infinite.

questions.question: To subtract one from a variable `x`, you can use the shortcut ____
questions.explanation: `x--` is the decrement operator.
